title: How to think about your financial life as a system
subtitle: Stop managing transactions. Start managing a system.

body.content: Most people manage their finances like they're fighting fires. Something comes up, they deal with it, they  check their account balance before a purchase and decide from that number whether to spend, they  have no real plan, just a series of reactions, this  approach works okay when things go well, it  fails badly when they don't.
The only number that tracks your actual progress

Net worth equals assets minus liabilities. That's it. Assets: bank accounts, investments, real estate equity, car value (roughly). Liabilities: mortgage balance, student loans, car loan, credit card balances. Subtract one from the other. That number is your score. It's the only metric that actually captures whether you're moving forward or backward over time.

body.label: Key concept
body.content: Net worth can be negative, and often is for people early in their careers with student loans. That's fine, what  matters is the direction and speed of change. A net worth growing by $10,000 per year is the sign of a working system. A net worth that's flat despite a good income is the sign of a problem somewhere.

Net worth tells you where you are. Cash flow tells you how fast you're moving. Monthly cash flow = income minus all expenses, including what you save and invest. Positive cash flow is the engine of wealth. Everything else, which index fund, which account type, which debt to pay first, is optimization of the engine. You need the engine first.

items: Earn more: salary negotiation, career growth, side income. The highest-leverage action for most people
Spend less: reduce fixed costs (housing, car) first; variable cuts are real but harder to sustain
Invest better: lower fees, better allocation, more consistency. Worth doing but less impactful than the first two

body.content: Most financial advice focuses on the third lever. Optimize your portfolio. Pick the right funds. Rebalance quarterly. That's real, but it's the smallest lever. A 25-year-old who negotiates a $15,000 raise will likely outperform a 25-year-old who optimizes their investment allocation by a factor of ten. Get the big things right first.

Here's how the system compounds over time. Higher income creates a bigger gap. That gap flows into investments. Investments grow and generate returns. Returns reduce the need for active income later. Lower financial pressure lets you take better career risks. Better career risks lead to higher income. Spin the flywheel and it gets easier. Stall it and everything stays hard.
Why small daily decisions matter less than you think

body.label: Real talk
body.content: Skipping daily lattes for a year saves about $1,000. Negotiating your rent down $200/month saves $2,400 a year with zero ongoing effort. Buying a $25,000 car instead of a $45,000 car saves $20,000 plus interest, money that can compound for decades, the  big fixed decisions, where you live, what you drive, what career you're in. dwarf the small daily ones. Stop optimizing the noise.

A working financial system is set up once and maintained occasionally. Not obsessed over daily. You automate savings. You rebalance annually. You check your net worth monthly. You revisit your budget when your life changes. The goal is a system that runs quietly in the background while you focus on actually living your life.

Think in systems, not transactions. Track net worth, not spending. Pull the biggest levers first: earn more, reduce fixed costs, automate savings. Set the system up. Maintain it occasionally. Then go live your life.
